title: Alzheimer disease: low choline acetyltransferase (nucleus basalis)
vignette: A 72-year-old woman comes to the office for a routine follow-up visit accompanied by her son. The patient has experienced progressive memory loss over the last 2 years. She now needs help with her daily activities, such as shopping and housekeeping. The patient's son says, "She used to be such an independent person, but now she needs help with most things. She even forgot my name the other day!" Medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Family history includes major depressive disorder in her father. MRI reveals diffuse cortical and hippocampal atrophy. Which of the following biochemical changes is most likely contributing to this patient's condition?

clues.text: progressive memory loss over the last 2 years
clues.note: Slow, progressive memory loss in an older adult is the prototypical presentation of Alzheimer disease, the most common cause of dementia.

clues.text: needs help with her daily activities
clues.note: Decline in instrumental activities of daily living (shopping, housekeeping) marks functional impairment - the threshold from cognitive impairment to dementia.
Forgetting a close family member's name reflects progressive anterograde memory loss, a hallmark of Alzheimer disease as it advances.

Diffuse cortical and HIPPOCAMPAL atrophy on MRI is the classic structural signature of Alzheimer disease. The hippocampus and the cholinergic nucleus basalis of Meynert are central to the memory deficit.

whatMatters: Progressive memory loss + cortical/hippocampal atrophy = Alzheimer disease. Its key biochemical abnormality is DECREASED acetylcholine from reduced CHOLINE ACETYLTRANSFERASE activity, most notably in the nucleus basalis of Meynert (and hippocampus). Answer A. This rationale underlies treatment with cholinesterase inhibitors.
strategy: Diagnose Alzheimer from the clinical + MRI picture, then recall its neurochemistry: low acetylcholine due to decreased choline acetyltransferase in the nucleus basalis of Meynert. Match the distractors to their diseases by neurotransmitter and nucleus: substantia nigra/dopamine = Parkinson; raphe/serotonin = mood/sleep; caudate = Huntington; locus ceruleus/norepinephrine = panic.

options.text: Decreased choline acetyltransferase activity in the nucleus basalis
Decreased dopa decarboxylase activity in the substantia nigra
Decreased tryptophan hydroxylase activity in the raphe nuclei
Increased acetylcholinesterase activity in the caudate nucleus
Increased dopamine beta-hydroxylase activity in the locus ceruleus

eliminations.label: Decreased dopa decarboxylase in substantia nigra - Parkinson
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: loss of dopaminergic neurons/enzymes in the substantia nigra reduces dopamine synthesis. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that causes Parkinson disease (and contributes to dementia with Lewy bodies), presenting with bradykinesia/rigidity/tremor - not the amnestic, cortical/hippocampal-atrophy dementia here. ✅ Correct if: parkinsonism from nigral dopamine loss.

eliminations.label: Decreased tryptophan hydroxylase in raphe nuclei - serotonin (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: tryptophan hydroxylase is the rate-limiting enzyme for serotonin synthesis in the raphe nuclei. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: reduced serotonin is implicated in depression, anxiety, and sleep disorders - not the cholinergic deficit of Alzheimer disease. The father's depression history is a distractor steering you to serotonin. ✅ Correct if: a primary mood/anxiety/sleep disorder, not amnestic dementia.

eliminations.label: Increased acetylcholinesterase in caudate nucleus - wrong direction & site
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: acetylcholinesterase degrades acetylcholine; the caudate atrophies in Huntington disease. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Alzheimer involves DECREASED acetylcholine SYNTHESIS (low choline acetyltransferase), not increased breakdown in the caudate; caudate atrophy points to Huntington (chorea), not amnestic dementia. ✅ Correct if: choreiform movement disorder with caudate atrophy (Huntington).

eliminations.label: Increased dopamine beta-hydroxylase in locus ceruleus - norepinephrine/panic
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: dopamine beta-hydroxylase converts dopamine to norepinephrine; the locus ceruleus is the main noradrenergic nucleus. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: locus ceruleus/noradrenergic changes are linked to panic disorder, not the cholinergic memory deficit of Alzheimer disease. ✅ Correct if: a noradrenergic/panic-related question.

reasoning: Progressive amnestic dementia with cortical and hippocampal atrophy = Alzheimer disease
Alzheimer's key biochemical defect is decreased acetylcholine
This is due to reduced choline acetyltransferase, most notably in the nucleus basalis of Meynert (and hippocampus)
-> decreased choline acetyltransferase in the nucleus basalis; answer A
mechanism: Alzheimer disease is the most common cause of dementia, presenting with slow, progressive anterograde memory loss and functional decline, with MRI showing generalized cortical and hippocampal atrophy and microscopic neurofibrillary tangles, senile (amyloid) plaques, and amyloid angiopathy. Its most important biochemical abnormality is a DECREASE in acetylcholine caused by deficiency of CHOLINE ACETYLTRANSFERASE, the synthetic enzyme, most prominent in the nucleus basalis of Meynert - a basal forebrain nucleus that projects widely to the neocortex - and in the hippocampus, both critical to memory and cognition. This cholinergic deficit is the rationale for symptomatic treatment with cholinesterase inhibitors, which reduce acetylcholine breakdown at the synapse. The distractors map to other disorders: substantia nigra/dopamine (Parkinson), raphe/serotonin (mood, sleep), caudate (Huntington), and locus ceruleus/norepinephrine (panic).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Neuro > Alzheimer disease: decreased ACh from low choline acetyltransferase in nucleus basalis of Meynert (and hippocampus); cortical/hippocampal atrophy; neurofibrillary tangles (tau), amyloid plaques; cholinesterase inhibitors + memantine.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The stem asks for the BIOCHEMICAL change. Diagnose Alzheimer, then recall the cholinergic deficit (low ChAT, nucleus basalis) rather than another neurotransmitter system.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on choline acetyltransferase, nucleus basalis of Meynert, dopa decarboxylase, tryptophan hydroxylase, dopamine beta-hydroxylase, or raphe/locus ceruleus? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: The father's depression nudges you toward serotonin/raphe (your pick), but this is an amnestic dementia - the deficit is cholinergic (low ChAT in the nucleus basalis), not serotonergic.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: Alzheimer = low ACh (decreased ChAT, nucleus basalis of Meynert); Parkinson = dopamine/substantia nigra; Huntington = caudate; depression/sleep = serotonin/raphe; panic = norepinephrine/locus ceruleus.

keywords.front: Key biochemical deficit in Alzheimer disease
keywords.back: Decreased acetylcholine - low choline acetyltransferase in nucleus basalis of Meynert
Basal forebrain cholinergic nucleus projecting to neocortex (memory/cognition)

keywords.front: Raphe nuclei / serotonin; locus ceruleus / norepinephrine

firstAid.detail: Neuro > decreased ACh; low choline acetyltransferase in nucleus basalis of Meynert & hippocampus; cortical/hippocampal atrophy.
firstAid.location: Neuro > decreased ACh; low choline acetyltransferase in nucleus basalis of Meynert & hippocampus; cortical/hippocampal atrophy.

firstAid.detail: Neuro > substantia nigra/dopamine (Parkinson), raphe/serotonin, caudate (Huntington), locus ceruleus/NE (panic).
firstAid.location: Neuro > substantia nigra/dopamine (Parkinson), raphe/serotonin, caudate (Huntington), locus ceruleus/NE (panic).

firstAid.detail: Neuro > neurofibrillary tangles (tau), amyloid plaques; cholinesterase inhibitors + memantine.
firstAid.location: Neuro > neurofibrillary tangles (tau), amyloid plaques; cholinesterase inhibitors + memantine.

memoryHook: Alzheimer runs low on ACh: the nucleus basalis stops making it (choline acetyltransferase down). That's why we block the esterase to keep what little ACh remains.
